---
title: "What a Dated Search Still Did Not Find: Wrist-Read Completion and Same-Day Load"
description: "Installment 7 could only classify two documentation questions as inferred tension — it audited the series' own prior text, not primary literature. This installment goes back to primary literature with those two specific questions and reports a dated, disclosed search: both remain unaddressed."
date: "2026-07-16"
series: "Foundation Stack"
installment: pillar2-008
pillar: methods
slug: same-day-load-and-wrist-completion
draft: false
---

::: {.callout-caution}
**Not medical advice. Educational use only. Data from published literature.**
:::

<!-- Opening hook is agent-drafted, per explicit author instruction ("go ahead" -> confirmed "write the opening hook", 2026-07-16) -- not a real authorial anecdote, disclosed here per the same practice used for installment 7's hook. -->

Closing out installment 7's interaction matrix, two rows sat differently from the rest. Every other cell traced back to a sentence in a trial or a consensus statement; these two traced back only to my own reasoning about what two separately-stated sentences might, read together, imply. Labeling them "inferred tension" felt honest at the time. It also felt like an IOU.

::: {.callout-note}
## New to this series?

This is **installment 8** of the [Foundation Stack](/index.html) — a PMID-backed methods series on adolescent **PPCS** (prolonged post-concussive symptoms) aerobic exercise prescription. This piece is short and narrow by design: it does not re-explain the whole series. If you have not read [installment 7](/posts/pillar2-007-prescription-path-topology/), the two questions below will make more sense with that context; skim its interaction-matrix summary first if you have five minutes.

**Two terms this piece is entirely about, in plain language:**

| Term | What it means here |
|------|----------------------|
| **T-001** | Does a wrist-worn heart-rate reading ever get treated differently, in the published literature, depending on which rule advances a prescription — versus a chest-strap or clinic reading? |
| **T-002** | Is there a published rule for what to do when a student-athlete has a symptom flare-up on the same day while being tracked on *both* the return-to-school and return-to-sport ladders at once? |

Installment 7 could not find either question answered by any of the series' first six pieces — it flagged both as its own inference, not a documented finding, and named them **T-001** and **T-002** in its "Tension register." This piece goes back to the primary literature itself to check.

**Abbreviations used here:** **BCTT** = Buffalo Concussion Treadmill Test; **RTL** / **RTP** = return to learn / return to sport (consensus documents often label sport steps **RTS**); **PMID** = PubMed identifier (linked below for every source).
:::

#### 1. Problem

Installment 7 audited how the series' first six pieces fit together, and found two places where it could not locate a stated rule — it could only combine two separately-documented facts into an inference of its own. It labeled both **inferred tension**, not a finding, and set them aside for exactly this reason: an audit of the series' own prior text is not the same thing as a search of primary literature.

**The result, stated up front:** a dated, disclosed search of primary literature — not just a re-read of the prior six pieces — still did not locate either rule. Both come back **checked silence**, not a new resolution. This is not a disappointing result to report quietly; it is a stronger epistemic status than installment 7 could claim on its own, and it changes what a clinician can honestly write on a documentation note today.

**T-001, in full.** [Installment 5](https://fs.guanglab.org/posts/pillar2-005-progression-after-first-prescription/) describes three ways to authorize the next increase in a prescription — one of them, **Logic C**, advances a session-dose prescription once the next scheduled session or clinic visit is completed. Installment 5 groups two variants under this logic (Micay's fixed percentage ramp and Kurowski's duration-titration protocol), but in both the trigger is the same event: a session marked complete. [Installment 6](https://fs.guanglab.org/posts/pillar2-006-wearable-hr-error-margins/) separately argues that a wrist-worn optical heart-rate reading disagrees with a reference reading by more than most prescribed heart-rate bands are wide — too wide to trust for confirming a session stayed inside a narrow band. Neither piece states whether those two facts combine: does a Logic C completion trigger depend on the session having been confirmed in-band, and does that confirmation's reliability change depending on which device did the reading? Installment 7 could only infer that it might.

**T-002, in full.** [Installment 4](https://fs.guanglab.org/posts/pillar2-004-school-vs-sport-staging/) describes two parallel step-ladders — one for returning to school, one for returning to sport — and installment 5 proposes, in its own words, that if either track generates a symptom escalation of more than 2/10 lasting more than an hour on a given day, advancement on *both* tracks should be deferred the next day. Installment 5 states in the same passage that this specific rule "has no published source." It is a proposed clinical-judgment default, not a citation.

**What this piece is not:** it does not run a new trial, and it does not overturn any already-settled part of installment 7's interaction matrix — in particular, the wrist-optical channel's exclusion from in-band adjudication (already stated-incompatible in installment 7's Figure 1a) is untouched. This piece asks a narrower, different question about each tension and reports what a real search found.

#### 2. Methods — search protocol {#methods-search-protocol}

Two independent search questions were run as their own literature-scout cycle (same GLP pipeline used for installment 6), dated **2026-07-15**:

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| Corpus | GLP research-assistant corpus (`concussion-research-rag`), queried via its RAG CLI |
| Model | `google/gemini-2.5-flash` for answer synthesis; near-miss chunks inspected directly |
| Independent check | Direct full-text keyword scan (`pypdf`) of the Amsterdam and Berlin consensus statements — not RAG retrieval alone |
| Not claimed | An exhaustive PubMed/Embase systematic review; grey literature or offline guideline supplements were not searched |

**T-001 query (verbatim):** *Does any adolescent PPCS or sport-related concussion aerobic exercise trial or consensus statement condition session-completion or progression-advancement decisions on the monitoring device class used to confirm in-band heart rate (wrist optical vs. chest strap)? Explicitly look for any paper that links wearable/device type to progression logic.* Searched deliberately beyond the four papers installment 7's own synthesis claim already used to establish the wrist-reliability argument.

**T-002 query (verbatim):** *Does any concussion consensus statement or aerobic exercise protocol give an explicit same-day rule for combined return-to-learn and return-to-sport symptom load — for example holding advancement on both tracks the next day if either shows symptom escalation? Beyond the high-level rule that full return-to-learn must precede unrestricted return-to-sport.* The Amsterdam and Berlin consensus documents were checked directly by keyword scan for this specific question, since both already supply the return-to-learn/return-to-sport sequencing gate itself (installment 7's Table A) but had not been searched for a same-day combined-load rule specifically.

Every PMID this search touched was independently re-verified rather than relayed from a prior receipt — the series' standing identifier-hygiene rule, adopted after installment 6 found a real PMID mismatch in its own source material by not skipping this step. No new PMID issues surfaced this pass.

**Continuity with installment 5's earlier combined-load scout.** Installment 5's own literature note already flagged the same-day RTL+RTP micro-rule as unpublished (Yang 2024 and Wingerson 2024 discuss related loads, not the hold-both-tracks rule). Those papers are not re-listed in Table A below — this piece re-checks the consensus primaries with an explicit same-day search string rather than repeating that earlier partial scout as table rows.

#### 3. Comparison — what a dated search located, and what it does not settle {#comparison-near-miss-audit}

*This table is not a positive-citation table like installment 7's Table A. Every source below was located; none of them settles either question — that "No" is this table's finding, not a footnote to it.*

| Tension | Source (PMID) | Claim tested | What the source actually says | Classification | Settles? |
|---------|----------------|--------------|--------------------------------|-----------------|----------|
| T-001 | Leddy/Buffalo RCT ([30715132](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30715132/)) | Device class named for home monitoring | "a provided Polar H7 Bluetooth Heart Rate Sensor" — a chest strap is specified in Methods | Near-miss — names a device class, but never conditions the advancement trigger on it | No |
| T-001 | Mercier et al. 2020 ([32024486](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32024486/)) | Device-conditioned progression | Adult PPCS aerobic-exercise *protocol* paper: prescription at 70–80% of symptom-exacerbation HR; target updated every 3 weeks via a repeat treadmill test | Near-miss — adult population outside the adolescent query frame; a retest cadence, not a device-class rule | No |
| T-001 | Del Rossi et al. 2020 ([32503036](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32503036/)) | Device-conditioned progression | Narrative review of early aerobic exercise after pediatric concussion; discusses symptom-gated dosing, not monitoring hardware | Near-miss — no device-class discussion at all | No |
| T-001 | Henke et al. 2020 ([31629334](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31629334/)) | Device-conditioned progression | Evidence review on early low-intensity exercise timing; recovery gated on symptom exacerbation | Near-miss — symptom gate, not a device-class rule | No |
| T-001 | Chizuk et al. 2023 ([36728783](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36728783/)) | Device-conditioned progression | Practical-management program for clinics without graded exercise testing; stop rule is symptom exacerbation (≥2-point rise), weekly re-evaluation | Near-miss — an explicit no-device pathway, still not a rule conditioning advancement on device class | No |
| T-002 | Amsterdam 2022 consensus ([37316210](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37316210/)) | Same-day combined-load rule | "RTL and RTS strategies can occur in parallel... complete full RTL before unrestricted RTS" — a macro sequencing gate, already in installment 7's Table A | False positive — a *sequencing* rule, not a *same-day symptom-load* rule | No |
| T-002 | Berlin 2017 consensus ([28446457](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28446457/)) | "same-day" keyword hit | "no same-day return to play" — this is the day-of-injury return-to-play ban, an unrelated rule | False positive — different question entirely | No |
| T-002 | Berlin 2017 consensus ([28446457](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28446457/)) | "concurrent" keyword hit | "published concurrently" (referring to companion systematic reviews) / "concurrent injury to the cervical spine" | False positive — both unrelated uses of the word | No |

#### 4. Disagreements {#disagreements}

Both tensions land on the same outcome, so there is one line, not a branch for each: **a dated, disclosed search found nothing that states either combination, in either direction.** That upgrades both from installment 7's *inferred tension* (an audit-only guess) to a **checked silence** (a searched, dated, and still-empty result) — a real epistemic gain, even though the content of the finding is "no." Neither promotes to installment 7's Figure 1a grid: that grid is reserved for combinations a source explicitly states as compatible or incompatible, and nothing here clears that bar.

This search deliberately extended beyond the four papers installment 7's own synthesis claim already used to establish the wrist-reliability argument — the question here is different (does any source condition the *advancement trigger* on device class), not a re-run of the wrist-reliability meta-picture. Installment 7's existing finding — that a wrist reading cannot adjudicate a narrow heart-rate band under any progression logic — stands untouched; this piece only checked whether any source separately ties that reliability question to *which* logic is running, and found nothing that does either way.

```{=html}
<figure id="fig-tension-closure">
<svg viewBox="0 0 720 150" width="100%" style="max-width:42rem;font-family:system-ui,sans-serif;background:#fafafa;border:1px solid #ddd;border-radius:4px">
  <rect x="10" y="10" width="700" height="55" fill="#fff8ec" stroke="#c9971f" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="6 3" rx="4"/>
  <text x="22" y="28" font-size="12" font-weight="600" fill="#7a5b00">T-001 — device class × progression logic · IT → checked silence</text>
  <text x="22" y="45" font-size="11" fill="#333">Installment 7: inferred tension (agent re-read only). This piece: checked silence — dated search, 2026-07-15, still found nothing.</text>
  <rect x="10" y="75" width="700" height="55" fill="#fff8ec" stroke="#c9971f" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="6 3" rx="4"/>
  <text x="22" y="93" font-size="12" font-weight="600" fill="#7a5b00">T-002 — same-day RTL+RTP combined-load rule · IT → checked silence</text>
  <text x="22" y="110" font-size="11" fill="#333">Installment 7: inferred tension (5's self-labeled unpublished convention). This piece: checked silence — same search date, still found nothing.</text>
  <text x="22" y="138" font-size="10" fill="#666">Neither status change is promoted into installment 7's Figure 1a grid.</text>
</svg>
<figcaption><strong>Figure — Tension register closed as checked silence.</strong> Not promoted to installment 7's Figure 1a: this is a confidence-tier upgrade in the working interaction-matrix note, not a new stated-compatible or stated-incompatible rule. Installment 7's own Figure 1b remains accurate as published — it only ever asserted that these two tensions exist, and never displayed a confidence tier on the public page, so nothing it said has gone stale.</figcaption>
</figure>
```

#### 5. SOP — what to write now {#sop-documentation-grammar}

*Sections 2–4 are deliberately short: there is no new citation to build a case around. What changes for practice is here.*

**1. Logic C sessions.** Record which path (per installment 6's device-class labels) supplied the heart-rate reading for each session, alongside the progression logic in force. A wrist-optical reading still cannot serve as evidence that a session was completed in-band — installment 6's bandwidth argument stands, and this search found no exception clause anywhere in primary literature for any progression logic. Nothing changes about what to do; what changes is that "no exception exists in the literature" is now a checked claim, not a guess.

**2. Same-day RTL+RTP hold.** Keep using installment 5's proposed rule — if either track generates a symptom escalation of more than 2/10 lasting more than an hour on a given day, defer advancement on both tracks the next day. The label on the documentation note must say **adopted clinical-judgment convention**, not a citation, exactly as installment 5 itself said. Add one line pointing to this piece's **Methods search date (2026-07-15)** so a reader can see the absence was checked, not assumed — not a private machine path, and not a citation pretending the rule has a PMID.

**3. Do not invent a new checkbox.** A confirmed-absent result is not grounds to add a new stated-incompatible cell to installment 7's matrix, or a new field to any documentation form. Silence stays silence; checking it more carefully does not turn it into a rule.

#### 6. Limitations

1. **Confirmed-absent is corpus-bound and dated, not proof of universal non-existence.** This search covered the GLP corpus plus a direct scan of the Amsterdam and Berlin consensus documents. It did not search grey literature, conference abstracts, or guideline supplements held offline. A future search could still find something this one did not.

2. **Scope stays narrow.** This piece checks exactly the two tensions installment 7 named — T-001 and T-002 — and no others. It does not reopen any already-resolved cell in installment 7's interaction matrix, and it does not address the narrow Leddy-2013 citation blocker installment 7 already scoped as non-urgent.

3. **Installment 7's live text is not being retroactively edited, and needs no errata.** Its Figure 1b only ever stated that these two tensions exist — it never displayed a confidence-tier label on the public page. Nothing installment 7 said has gone stale; only the private working matrix's internal confidence label was upgraded. Correcting a public claim and updating a private audit trail are not the same action, and only the first would require a visible erratum.

4. **No new trial comparisons.** This piece does not test whether device class actually affects outcomes, or whether a same-day combined-load hold changes recovery time. It only checks whether the published literature has ever stated a rule for either — and reports, honestly, that it has not.

---

**Series:** Foundation Stack · **pillar2-008** · **Pillar:** methods · wrist-read completion and same-day load
